rationales.security: He fixates on whether 'Amma took her evening medication yesterday' and notes 'I just worry,' showing strong concern with keeping family circumstances safe and under control.
rationales.benevolence: His attention stays on close family members, worrying about Amma's medication and noticing Ananya 'looking drained' while quietly caring for her at dinner.

rationales.stimulation: He rejects the fintech offer and is stung by the claim that he is 'too afraid to try something new,' which points to avoidance of novelty rather than seeking it.
rationales.security: He defends staying because 'my PF is solid' and 'Amma and Appa are right here,' grounding the choice in predictable footing and family proximity.
He wants 'another opinion' before a second surgery and admits he is 'scared of Appa going through all that pain again,' showing a protective response to possible harm.
rationales.benevolence: His concern centers on Appa's wellbeing, especially the fear of him 'going through all that pain again,' which reflects care for a close family member.

rationales.tradition: He dwells on Sunday puja, 'the smell of camphor,' and his grandfather explaining 'some story from the epics,' showing attachment to inherited family and religious practices.

rationales.stimulation: He turns away from the fintech move after checking layoffs and unstable equity, treating the more novel path as too risky to pursue.
rationales.security: He focuses on 'two rounds of layoffs,' weak healthcare, upcoming 'college fees,' and rising medical bills, making the decision around dependable protection for the future.
rationales.benevolence: He frames the choice around responsibilities to 'a daughter whose college fees start in two years' and 'parents whose medical bills are only going up,' then spends the evening helping Ananya with physics.

rationales.tradition: Making 'Paati's rasam recipe' immediately brings back 'Her kitchen in Mylapore' and childhood summers, tying the moment to family continuity and inherited practice.

rationales.security: He worries that the old path led to 'something stable' and asks 'What does film lead to?', showing concern about whether his son's choice will provide dependable footing.
rationales.tradition: He frames engineering as part of a family line: 'My father was an engineer. His father was a civil servant. There was a path.'
rationales.benevolence: Even while conflicted, he says 'I just don't want him to stop talking to me about it,' prioritizing preserving the relationship with his son.

rationales.achievement: He 'accepted the payments integration lead role' and notes that after 'Seventeen years' he is still the person trusted to get important work done.
rationales.security: He agrees to explore 'actual structured' film programs and says he cannot just say 'go for it with no plan,' emphasizing a controlled, dependable route.
rationales.benevolence: He tells Vikram 'I'd look into some programs with him,' showing active support for his son's next step even while worried.

rationales.achievement: He takes pride in having 'spent months building that credibility' through preparation and in earning recognition for strong performance.
rationales.power: He relishes that 'the room orients toward you' and that people recognize he 'belong[s] at the front,' which is explicit satisfaction with influence and standing.

He defends a structure where 'someone has to manage the flow, assign speaking order, handle Q&A' and is offended at being treated as 'someone you go around,' showing strong investment in authority and control.

rationales.achievement: He insists the paper is 'my ticket to the next conference' and wants it as proof that he can produce something significant on his own.
rationales.power: He says 'My name on that paper matters' and worries that sharing credit would stop him from being seen as the person 'at the front of a room,' reflecting status-focused motivation.
rationales.conformity: He resists the request to share authorship for 'departmental cohesion,' treating the collective expectation as an unwelcome constraint rather than something to accommodate.

rationales.achievement: He is deeply bothered by the idea of a conference program with 'three names where mine used to be alone,' because he wants his work visibly credited.
rationales.power: He reacts strongly to the idea that not everything needs 'your stamp on it' and to losing the solo place he had 'at a table you set,' signaling attachment to personal standing and ownership.

rationales.stimulation: He calls the Monterrey assignment 'Finally something different on the calendar' and says the office routine felt 'like wearing a shirt that's too tight,' showing clear pursuit of novelty over sameness.
The empty warehouse feels 'like possibility' and 'the first page of a notebook,' showing excitement for a new setting and challenge.
rationales.hedonism: He highlights sensory enjoyment in 'carnitas,' the hotel balcony, and how Lucía's possible visit made 'the whole day feel complete,' treating pleasure as part of the day's value.

rationales.stimulation: He is 'already counting days until the next Monterrey trip,' with the ordinary office view feeling deadening by comparison, which shows a strong pull toward the less routine option.
He cannot relate to a 'ten-year plan,' says 'Keep moving' is closer to his own way of living, and describes himself as 'allergic to contentment,' all of which favor change and variety.
rationales.security: Tomás's stable path leaves him feeling 'from a different species' and makes the city feel 'gray,' showing discomfort with predictability rather than attraction to it.

rationales.stimulation: He says 'I always think about what's next,' signaling an ongoing pull toward new directions even while staying on the current project.
rationales.achievement: He emphasizes 'I've never left a project half-finished in my life,' framing himself around reliably delivering work to completion.

rationales.stimulation: He says 'variety doesn't always require a boarding pass,' explicitly treating the night out as welcome novelty.
rationales.hedonism: He took Lucía dancing at 'that new salsa club' and stayed until '1am,' centering the entry on enjoyment and a fun sensory experience.

rationales.tradition: He says the family house was 'just a building' and contrasts his relatives' sense of places as 'anchors' with his own view of them as 'way stations,' rejecting the inherited attachment others hold.

rationales.stimulation: He says the construction phase is 'always my favorite part' and that once things run smoothly he gets 'restless again,' showing a strong preference for novelty and movement.
rationales.achievement: Watching 'something you started take shape' feels good to him, linking satisfaction to building and advancing a visible project.

rationales.stimulation: He is drained by meetings focused only on 'risk, risk, risk' and wishes people were asking 'what new routes we could try' and 'what experiments might be worth running.'
rationales.hedonism: He treats the canyon trip and Lucía as what is 'rescuing' him and says he needs those 'counterweights,' showing active pursuit of enjoyable relief.
rationales.security: He reacts negatively to hours of 'backup plans and contingencies and worst-case scenarios,' experiencing that protective mindset as 'soul-crushing' rather than reassuring.

rationales.self_direction: He imagines a life where he would 'learn a language, live somewhere for a few months, move on' and maybe write, which reflects a self-authored path shaped by his own interests.
rationales.stimulation: He wants 'exploratory travel' rather than vacation travel and imagines repeatedly moving on to new places and languages.
rationales.hedonism: The entry lingers on the picnic, 'marzipan candies I like,' and sitting together 'just watching shadows move across the canyon walls,' centering present enjoyment.

rationales.self_direction: He keeps questioning why others see him as strange for 'not wanting to settle down' and considers whether this is simply 'how I'm wired,' pointing to living by an internally defined pattern.
rationales.stimulation: He says each project feels 'genuinely different' and that doing one thing for twenty-five years would feel like 'suffocation,' which clearly favors novelty and change.
rationales.security: The idea of staying in one domain for decades triggers 'a kind of panic,' showing active discomfort with steadiness and long-term sameness.
